display_summary.headline: Full storm. No calm domains in sight.
display_summary.narrative: Every sector is under pressure — AI Research and Business Adoption are feeding the system with job-loss data and headcount-cut mandates, while Social Impact and Entry-Level Erosion sit at the eye wall, with Yale's career-ladder findings and graduate unemployment rising twice as fast as the broader workforce driving the deepest vortex in the ring.

domains.label: AI Research
domains.sentiment_basis: The research is increasingly alarming. Anthropic's labor market study, Brookings analysis, and MIT data all point toward measurable displacement accelerating. Geoffrey Hinton's warnings have moved from theoretical to empirically grounded.

news.headline: Anthropic publishes most detailed map yet of which jobs AI is actively performing vs. could perform
news.summary: Anthropic's labor market study models a 'Great Recession for white-collar workers' as a plausible scenario — a doubling of unemployment in the top AI-exposure quartile from 3% to 6% — while noting current data shows no catastrophic macro signal yet.

news.headline: Brynjolfsson et al.: 6–16% fall in employment in AI-exposed occupations among workers aged 22–25
news.summary: Academic research finds a significant drop in employment among young workers in AI-exposed roles — attributed primarily to a slowdown in hiring rather than an increase in layoffs, meaning AI is quietly closing entry points rather than visibly eliminating existing jobs.

news.headline: MIT study: 11.7% of current jobs could already be automated using existing AI technologies
news.summary: An MIT study found that over one in ten jobs could be automated today with current AI — a conservative baseline estimate that rises significantly when factoring in near-term model capability improvements.

news.headline: Geoffrey Hinton warns 2026 will bring a new wave of AI job losses — 'capabilities to replace many, many jobs'
news.summary: The 'Godfather of AI' said AI will move from replacing call center workers to replacing most white-collar roles, noting AI now handles hour-long coding projects and predicts month-long engineering projects within years.

news.headline: WEF predicts AI displaces 92 million jobs — but 170 million new roles emerge by 2030
news.summary: The World Economic Forum's forecast suggests net job creation globally, but the displacement-to-creation timeline gap — and geographic/skill mismatch — means the transition will be highly unequal across regions and education levels.

domains.label: Business Adoption
domains.sentiment_basis: Enterprise AI adoption is accelerating restructuring. JPMorgan, Klarna, and others have confirmed AI-driven displacement. 42% of companies cite AI specifically as a headcount reduction tool. The corporate efficiency narrative is winning over the augmentation narrative.

news.headline: JPMorgan CEO confirms AI-driven workforce displacement already underway — fewer hires expected
news.summary: Jamie Dimon confirmed at the Hill & Valley Forum that JPMorgan has already displaced workers due to AI, and expects the bank to hire fewer people in coming years. He warned AI disruption 'may be quicker' than past technological transitions.
news.source: Michigan Journal of Economics / Hill & Valley Forum

news.headline: 42% of companies surveyed want to use AI specifically to cut headcount
news.summary: Survey data reveals that nearly half of companies are actively targeting headcount reduction — not just efficiency gains — as a primary motivation for AI adoption, with 66% of managers and 48% planning workforce reductions.

news.headline: Klarna rehires human agents in 2026 after AI chatbot replacement proves insufficient
news.summary: Klarna, which replaced 700 customer service workers with an AI chatbot in 2024, began rehiring human agents in 2026 — a notable reversal that signals the limits of full AI replacement in customer-facing roles.

news.headline: 78,000 tech workers laid off globally in early 2026 — nearly half attributed to AI
news.summary: Global tech layoffs reached 78,000 in the first months of 2026, with approximately half of those terminations linked to AI-driven efficiency measures — the highest concentration of AI-attributed layoffs yet recorded.

news.headline: 71% of European firms reconsidering job responsibilities due to AI — but few eliminating outright
news.summary: European data shows most firms are restructuring roles rather than eliminating them outright — a more measured adoption pace than US counterparts, partly driven by stronger labor protections and EU AI Act compliance requirements.

domains.label: Software Dev
domains.sentiment_basis: Software engineering is the most directly impacted knowledge-work domain. Junior developer roles are compressing rapidly. Agentic coding is automating entry-level tasks. The 'supervisory engineer' model is emerging but it doesn't absorb the same headcount.

news.headline: GitHub 14x commit growth signals agentic AI is absorbing junior developer output at scale
news.summary: The 14x increase in automated commits on GitHub signals that AI agents are now performing tasks that previously required junior and mid-level developers — compressing the traditional entry-level software engineering pipeline.

news.headline: Developers shift from implementation to architectural review — role exists but pipeline shrinks
news.summary: The emerging 'supervisory engineer' model — where developers review and guide AI-generated code rather than write it — represents a real role transformation, but one that requires fewer total headcount than the previous model.

news.headline: Geoffrey Hinton: AI will handle month-long software engineering projects within years — 'very few people needed'
news.summary: Hinton specifically warned that AI has moved from minute-length to hour-length coding tasks and will reach month-long engineering projects within years, at which point 'there will be very few people needed' in the field.

news.headline: MLOps, agent orchestration, and AI product management emerging as high-demand engineering roles
news.summary: While traditional software engineering headcount compresses, new roles in ML operations, AI agent orchestration, and AI product management are growing — concentrated in companies building AI-native products rather than adopting AI into legacy stacks.

news.headline: AI-generated code now 22% of merged enterprise code — security review burden shifts to humans
news.summary: As AI authorship of production code crosses 22%, security review, architectural governance, and bias auditing are emerging as the primary human responsibilities in software engineering pipelines.

domains.label: Social Impact
domains.sentiment_basis: Severe. Identity disruption, anxiety, entry-level career closure, and a K-shaped economy are all documented. The psychological cost of uncertainty is compounding the economic cost of displacement.

news.headline: Yale: AI is closing entry-level career pathways before they begin — skill ladder being pulled up
news.summary: Yale researchers find that agentic AI's primary workforce impact is not mass layoffs but the quiet elimination of entry-level roles — cutting off the skill-building progression that allows workers to develop into senior positions.

news.headline: K-shaped economy accelerating: AI-era winners and losers diverging faster than prior tech transitions
news.summary: Analysts describe a 'K-shaped economy' where workers in AI-adjacent roles enjoy rising opportunities while workers in shrinking sectors face tighter resources — with the divide widening faster than historical technology transitions.

news.headline: Gallup: 18% of U.S. workers believe AI may eliminate their job within five years — 23% in AI-adopting orgs
news.summary: Nearly 1 in 5 U.S. workers considers their job at existential risk from AI within five years, rising to nearly 1 in 4 inside organizations actively deploying AI. The anxiety is measurable and growing.

news.headline: Economists predict 'jobless boom' in 2026 — productivity gains without proportional hiring
news.summary: Multiple economic forecasters are projecting a 'jobless boom': AI drives productivity and corporate earnings higher while hiring remains suppressed — a scenario where GDP grows but employment does not recover proportionally.

news.headline: BLS February 2026 report: employers shed 92,000 jobs, unemployment ticks to 4.4%
news.summary: The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported 92,000 jobs shed in February 2026 and unemployment rising to 4.4%, with some companies explicitly attributing layoffs to AI efficiency initiatives.

domains.label: Regulation
domains.sentiment_basis: More active than expected. China's court rulings are a global precedent. California moved first in the US. EU AI Act classifies employment AI as high-risk. But US federal protection remains absent and enforcement lags deployment by years.

news.headline: Chinese courts rule companies cannot fire workers solely to replace them with AI — landmark precedent
news.summary: Courts in Hangzhou and Beijing ruled that AI adoption is a strategic business choice, not an unforeseeable circumstance — meaning companies must retrain, reassign, or continue employing workers displaced by AI. The rulings were timed to Workers' Day and represent the first binding legal protection of this kind globally.

news.headline: Governor Newsom signs California EO N-6-26 mandating AI labor market reporting
news.summary: California's executive order — the first of its kind in the US — mandates state agencies and industry partners to report on AI labor market statistics, best practices, and WARN Act update recommendations. It also directs review of collective bargaining processes incorporating AI.

news.headline: EU AI Act classifies AI used in hiring, performance management, and termination as high-risk
news.summary: Under the EU AI Act fully applicable from August 2026, AI systems used in employment decisions — hiring, promotion, performance management, termination — are classified as high-risk, with employers directly liable regardless of third-party tool use.

news.headline: US has no federal AI employment protection — WARN Act does not prohibit AI-driven terminations
news.summary: The Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act requires only 60 days' notice before mass layoffs. It does not prohibit AI-driven terminations or require negotiation, retraining, or reassignment — leaving US workers with no equivalent to the Chinese or EU protections.

news.headline: UNI Global Union argues digital retraining should be a labor right in the AI era
news.summary: UNI Global Union, representing over 20 million workers globally, is pushing for employer-funded upskilling as a contractual labor right — not a goodwill gesture — with unions shifting strategy from resisting automation to shaping its implementation.

domains.label: Entry-Level Erosion
domains.sentiment_basis: The most structurally alarming domain. The career ladder is being dismantled from the bottom. Graduate unemployment is rising twice as fast as the overall workforce. New roles exist but require skills that entry-level workers haven't had the chance to build.

news.headline: Graduate unemployment rising nearly twice as fast as broader workforce since 2022
news.summary: MIT Technology Review analysis of BLS data shows that while aggregate AI labor market impact remains limited, unemployment among recent graduates is climbing to nearly 6% — rising at roughly twice the rate of the broader workforce.

news.headline: US tech layoffs in 2026 hit entry-level white-collar roles hardest
news.summary: The 1.1 million US layoffs in 2025 and continuing 2026 cuts have disproportionately impacted tech and entry-level white-collar jobs — with data entry, basic financial analysis, and routine administrative work seeing the sharpest contraction.

news.headline: Brookings: 3.5 million workers with alternative-route skills face high AI exposure and low adaptive capacity
news.summary: Brookings identifies a specific at-risk cohort: 3.5 million workers who built skills through alternative routes — not four-year degrees — who face both high AI displacement exposure and limited pathways to adapt, representing the most vulnerable group in the transition.

news.headline: New AI roles exist — but require experience entry-level workers can no longer accumulate
news.summary: Mercor research identifies growing demand for AI auditors, governance specialists, clinical AI validators, and domain specialists — but these roles require deep field expertise that can only be gained through the junior roles AI is simultaneously eliminating.

news.headline: AI skills shift paper: observable reality now shows 6–16% employment drop in exposed occupations for ages 22–25
news.summary: Academic research tracking LLM-era labor impacts finds measurable, statistically significant employment declines in AI-exposed occupations for workers aged 22–25 — the exact cohort entering the workforce for the first time.

news.headline: Treasury considering tax-free treatment for employer-sponsored AI retraining — but no mandate
news.summary: The US Department of the Treasury is considering making employer-sponsored AI training tax-free — a positive signal, but one that remains voluntary and falls far short of the binding retraining obligations emerging in China and under EU law.
